88	class HCGRCState(TypedDict):
89	    """
90	    Canonical platform state. run_id propagates to all four stores:
91	      - MLflow  (tags.run_id)
92	      - Phoenix (trace attribute run_id)
93	      - PROV-DM (activity IRI contains run_id)
94	      - PostgresSaver (thread_id column)
95	
96	    See RUNBOOK.md for cross-store query procedures.
97	    """
98	
99	    # ── Identity ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
100	    run_id: str  # UUID4, set at graph entry, propagated everywhere
101	    thread_id: str  # LangGraph thread ID (== run_id for single-run threads)
102	    phase: Phase
103	
104	    # ── Gate tracking ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
105	    # Merge reducer: {**a, **b} ensures concurrent gate writes are safe by
106	    # construction. gate_coordinator_node is the canonical writer, but the
107	    # reducer protects against any future path that returns a partial update.
108	    # Fixes: ADR-0015 §75 serial guarantee (F1) + silent overwrite risk (F4).
109	    gate_status: Annotated[dict[GateID, GateStatus], lambda a, b: {**a, **b}]
110	
111	    # ── Failure events (written by gate nodes on rejection) ───────────────────
112	    # Agent Evolution monitors this store for routing re-optimization signals.
113	    failure_events: Annotated[list[dict[str, Any]], lambda a, b: a + b]
114	
115	    # ── Research artifacts (append-only) ──────────────────────────────────────
116	    hypotheses: Annotated[list[dict[str, Any]], lambda a, b: a + b]
117	    findings: Annotated[list[dict[str, Any]], lambda a, b: a + b]
118	
119	    # ── Data split record ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
120	    # Un-reduced (last-write-wins) scalars. Written by exactly one node per phase —
121	    # data_split_node in Phase 0, DataStewardAgent in Phase 1 — never concurrently,
122	    # so last-write-wins is safe by sequencing, not by a reducer (#212). Immutable
123	    # after Gate 1. If these ever become concurrently written, add a reducer.
124	    data_split_manifest_hash: str | None  # SHA-256 of the raw manifest file
125	    data_split_seed: int | None  # derived from manifest hash (int.from_bytes)
126	    data_split_verified: bool  # True after idempotency assertion passes
127	
128	    # ── Phase 1 execution tracking ────────────────────────────────────────────
129	    # phase_1_ready: True after data pipeline writes validated splits to disk.
130	    # exploratory_complete: True after all P1-P5 nodes complete (or graceful stub).
131	    phase_1_ready: bool
132	    exploratory_complete: bool
133	
134	    # ── Data pipeline provenance ──────────────────────────────────────────────
135	    # Written by EmbeddingAgent after embedding run completes. Required at Gate 2
136	    # to confirm which model produced the embeddings used in exploratory analysis.
137	    # Keys: model_name, model_hash, corpus_dvc_hash, timestamp_utc
138	    embedding_manifest: dict[str, Any] | None
139	
140	    # ── Exploratory analysis artifacts (append, deduped) ──────────────────────
141	    # eda_artifacts: file paths to all EXP_* outputs from P1-P5 nodes.
142	    # Each node appends its artifact paths. Gate 3 verifies all 5 agents present.
143	    # Deduped on merge so a Gate-2 reject → re-run-exploratory loop cannot
144	    # accumulate the same artifact path repeatedly (#235). Membership tested against
145	    # a set so the merge is O(n+m), not O(n*m), as the artifact list grows (pass-5).
146	    # eda_agent_statuses: per-agent completion records.
147	    # Keys: agent_id, status ("completed"|"stub_pending"|"failed"), note, timestamp_utc
148	    eda_artifacts: Annotated[list[str], _merge_artifacts]
149	    eda_agent_statuses: Annotated[list[dict[str, Any]], lambda a, b: a + b]
150	
151	    # ── Formalized hypothesis set (append, deduped by id) ─────────────────────
152	    # Written by HypothesisFormalizerAgent before Gate 2.
153	    # Each entry is a FormalHypothesis dict (see src/agents/hypothesis_formalizer).
154	    # Deduped by hypothesis id (latest wins) so a Gate-2 reject → re-formalize loop
155	    # cannot accumulate duplicate hypotheses into the set Gate 2 locks (pass-4 #2).
156	    hypothesis_set: Annotated[list[dict[str, Any]], lambda a, b: _merge_hypotheses(a, b)]
157	
158	    # ── Orchestrator run-start grounding ──────────────────────────────────────
159	    # Produced by t00_orchestrator_node via the reasoning_client (Tier 2). The
160	    # orchestrator reasons about the run *inside the graph* rather than the work
161	    # being done by hand outside the framework (ADR-0016 — the agency contract).
162	    # None when the reasoning backend was unavailable (graceful degradation).
163	    run_grounding: str | None
164	
165	    # ── Escalation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
166	    escalation_issue_number: int | None  # GitHub issue number if parked
167	    escalation_reason: str | None
168	
169	    # ── LLM messages (for agent traces) ──────────────────────────────────────
170	    messages: Annotated[list, add_messages]
171	
172	    # ── Provenance ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
173	    prov_activities: Annotated[list[dict[str, Any]], lambda a, b: a + b]
174

1	"""
2	HC-GRC main LangGraph graph — Phase 0 + Phase 1 exploratory subgraph.
3	
4	Graph topology (Phase 0 + Phase 1):
5	
6	  orchestrator
7	      ↓
8	  data_split          ← SHA-256 seeded 70/15/15 split (synthetic in Phase 0)
9	      ↓
10	  gate_1              ← interrupt() — pre-data checkpoint
11	      ↓ [approved]
12	  data_pipeline       ← DataAcquisitionAgent → DataCurationAgent
13	      ↓                  → DataStewardAgent → EmbeddingAgent
14	  exploratory_phase   ← P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P5 (sequential; TODO: Send() fan-out)
15	      ↓
16	  hypothesis_formalize ← HypothesisFormalizerAgent
17	      ↓
18	  gate_2              ← interrupt() — SAP lock, hypothesis pre-registration
19	      ↓ [approved]
20	  confirmatory_entry  ← Phase 2 stub (route_after_gate_2 → "confirmatory_entry")
21	      ↓ [rejected]
22	  exploratory_phase   ← loop back to re-run EDA with revised hypotheses
23	
24	  gate_3, gate_4, gate_5 are Phase 2 mechanism work — their node functions live
25	  in nodes/gates.py but are NOT registered in this Phase 0/1 graph (they would be
26	  unreachable). They are wired into the confirmatory subgraph in Phase 2.
27	
28	Gate nodes use interrupt() to surface proposals to the operator.
29	All gate decisions are written by gate_coordinator (serialized, no concurrent writes).
30	
31	Per ADR-0015: run_id propagates through thread_config to PostgresSaver
32	and to all four observability stores.
33	"""
34	
35	from __future__ import annotations
36	
37	from typing import Any
38	
39	from langgraph.graph import END, StateGraph
40	from langgraph.graph.state import CompiledStateGraph
41	from langgraph.types import Command
42	
43	from .checkpointer import configured_checkpointer
44	from .infrastructure.observability.phoenix_setup import (
45	    bootstrap_observability,
46	    run_trace_context,
47	)
48	from .nodes.data_split import data_split_node
49	from .nodes.gates import gate_1_node, gate_2_node
50	from .nodes.orchestrator import route_after_gate_1, route_after_gate_2, t00_orchestrator_node
51	from .nodes.phase1 import (
52	    confirmatory_entry_node,
53	    data_pipeline_node,
54	    exploratory_phase_node,
55	    hypothesis_formalize_node,
56	)
57	from .state import HCGRCState, initial_state
58	
59	# Upper bound on graph super-steps per run. Bounds the Gate-2-rejected loop back
60	# to exploratory analysis so a non-converging run fails loudly (#179).
61	GRAPH_RECURSION_LIMIT = 50
62	
63	# Resilience is layered, NOT via node RetryPolicy (pass-4 #15): the reasoning_client
64	# retries transient LLM failures with backoff; the agent nodes isolate per-agent
65	# failures as a 'failed' status + failure_event (so one agent's error neither
66	# crashes the run nor is retried into a re-executed SAP violation); the checkpointer
67	# uses a self-healing pool; and GRAPH_RECURSION_LIMIT bounds loops. A node-level
68	# RetryPolicy was dead — the nodes catch their own exceptions before LangGraph
69	# would ever retry — and is therefore not used.
70	
71	# ── Graph builder ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72	
73	def build_graph(checkpointer=None) -> CompiledStateGraph:
74	    """
75	    Build and compile the HC-GRC LangGraph graph.
76	
77	    Args:
78	        checkpointer: LangGraph checkpointer (MemorySaver for Phase 0/1,
79	                      PostgresSaver for production). If None, graph runs
80	                      without persistence (stateless — for unit tests only).
81	
82	    Returns:
83	        Compiled LangGraph graph.
84	    """
85	    builder = StateGraph(HCGRCState)
86	
87	    # ── Phase 0 nodes ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
88	    builder.add_node("orchestrator", t00_orchestrator_node)
89	
90	    # data_split_node needs synthetic_control_ids in Phase 0.
91	    # In Phase 1, DataStewardAgent writes real splits to disk.
92	    # Phase 0 wrapper: synthetic IDs matching SCF scale for gate dry-runs.
93	    def _data_split_phase0(state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
94	        synthetic_ids = [f"SCF-CTRL-{i:04d}" for i in range(1400)]
95	        return data_split_node(state, synthetic_control_ids=synthetic_ids)
96	
97	    builder.add_node("data_split", _data_split_phase0)
98	    builder.add_node("gate_1", gate_1_node)
99	
100	    # ── Phase 1 nodes ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
101	    builder.add_node("data_pipeline", data_pipeline_node)
102	    builder.add_node("exploratory_phase", exploratory_phase_node)
103	    builder.add_node("hypothesis_formalize", hypothesis_formalize_node)
104	    builder.add_node("gate_2", gate_2_node)
105	
106	    # ── Phase 2 stub ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
107	    # gate_3/4/5 are NOT registered here: they have no inbound edges in Phase 0/1
108	    # and would be dead/unreachable nodes (#213). They are wired into the
109	    # confirmatory subgraph in Phase 2. Their node functions live in nodes/gates.py.
110	    builder.add_node("confirmatory_entry", confirmatory_entry_node)
111	
112	    # ── Edges — Phase 0 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
113	    builder.set_entry_point("orchestrator")
114	    builder.add_edge("orchestrator", "data_split")
115	    builder.add_edge("data_split", "gate_1")
116	
117	    builder.add_conditional_edges(
118	        "gate_1",
119	        route_after_gate_1,
120	        {
121	            "phase_1_entry": "data_pipeline",  # approved → Phase 1 data pipeline
122	            "end": END,
123	        },
124	    )
125	
126	    # ── Edges — Phase 1 exploratory ───────────────────────────────────────────
127	    builder.add_edge("data_pipeline", "exploratory_phase")
128	    builder.add_edge("exploratory_phase", "hypothesis_formalize")
129	    builder.add_edge("hypothesis_formalize", "gate_2")
130	
131	    builder.add_conditional_edges(
132	        "gate_2",
133	        route_after_gate_2,
134	        {
135	            "confirmatory_entry": "confirmatory_entry",  # approved → Phase 2
136	            "exploratory_entry": "exploratory_phase",    # rejected → re-run EDA
137	            "end": END,                                   # deferred → park
138	        },
139	    )
140	
141	    # ── Phase 2 stub edge ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
142	    # confirmatory_entry → gate_3 → gate_4 → gate_5 will be wired in Phase 2.
143	    # For now, confirmatory_entry routes to END so the graph compiles.
144	    builder.add_edge("confirmatory_entry", END)
145	
146	    # ── Compile ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
147	    compile_kwargs: dict[str, Any] = {}
148	    if checkpointer is not None:
149	        compile_kwargs["checkpointer"] = checkpointer
150	
151	    return builder.compile(**compile_kwargs)
152	
153	
154	# ── Convenience runners ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
155	
156	def run_phase0_synthetic(run_id: str | None = None, checkpointer=None) -> dict[str, Any]:
157	    """
158	    Execute one complete Phase 0 synthetic run.
159	
160	    The graph will pause at Gate 1 and wait for operator input.
161	    This function is used in the governance dry-run.
162	
163	    For tests that need to skip the interrupt, use the graph's
164	    .invoke() with a pre-configured Command resume.
165	    """
166	    # interrupt() is a no-op without a checkpointer — the gate could not pause for
167	    # the operator. Default to the configured backend so the runner actually parks
168	    # at the gate (#200/#226).
169	    checkpointer = checkpointer or configured_checkpointer()
170	    graph = build_graph(checkpointer=checkpointer)
171	    state = initial_state(run_id=run_id)
172	    bootstrap_observability()
173	    # Explicit recursion_limit bounds the Gate-2-rejected → exploratory loop so a
174	    # run that never converges terminates with GraphRecursionError instead of
175	    # looping unbounded (#179).
176	    thread_config = {
177	        "configurable": {"thread_id": state["run_id"]},
178	        "recursion_limit": GRAPH_RECURSION_LIMIT,
179	    }
180	    with run_trace_context(state["run_id"]):
181	        result = graph.invoke(state, config=thread_config)
182	    return result
183	
184	
185	def resume_run(run_id: str, decision: str, rationale: str, checkpointer,
186	               *, gate_id: str, checkpoint_id: str | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]:
187	    """
188	    Resume a run parked at a gate interrupt with the operator's decision.
189	
190	    The runners invoke the graph once and return when a gate's interrupt() parks
191	    it; this is the other half of the loop (#162). The operator's decision is
192	    delivered via the ADR-0014 governance channel and injected here as the
193	    interrupt's resume value, so the gate node observes
194	    {'gate_id': ..., 'decision': ..., 'rationale': ...} and continues.
195	
196	    gate_id (the gate the operator was deciding on, from the ADR-0014 channel) is
197	    REQUIRED so the gate-correlation guard in _run_gate can reject a stale/misrouted
198	    decision targeted at a gate other than the one currently parked (pass-4 #3). It
199	    must be the OPERATOR's intended gate, not auto-derived from the parked gate —
200	    auto-deriving would make the guard always match and thus a no-op (pass-9). The
201	    earlier optional default silently disabled the guard whenever a caller omitted it.
202	
203	    Requires the SAME checkpointer the run was started with (thread_id == run_id).
204	    """
205	    graph = build_graph(checkpointer=checkpointer)
206	    bootstrap_observability()
207	    config: dict[str, Any] = {
208	        "configurable": {"thread_id": run_id},
209	        "recursion_limit": GRAPH_RECURSION_LIMIT,
210	    }
211	    if checkpoint_id:
212	        config["configurable"]["checkpoint_id"] = checkpoint_id
213	    resume_value: dict[str, Any] = {"decision": decision, "rationale": rationale,
214	                                    "gate_id": gate_id}
215	    with run_trace_context(run_id):
216	        return graph.invoke(Command(resume=resume_value), config=config)
217	
218	
219	def run_phase1_dry_run(run_id: str | None = None, checkpointer=None) -> dict[str, Any]:
220	    """
221	    Execute a Phase 1 dry run through Gate 2.
222	
223	    Gate 1 and Gate 2 will pause for operator input. Data pipeline and P1-P5
224	    nodes will record stub_pending statuses (NotImplementedError caught). This
225	    verifies the Phase 1 graph topology before real SCF data is loaded.
226	    """
227	    checkpointer = checkpointer or configured_checkpointer()
228	    graph = build_graph(checkpointer=checkpointer)
229	    state = initial_state(run_id=run_id)
230	    bootstrap_observability()
231	    # Explicit recursion_limit bounds the Gate-2-rejected → exploratory loop so a
232	    # run that never converges terminates with GraphRecursionError instead of
233	    # looping unbounded (#179).
234	    thread_config = {
235	        "configurable": {"thread_id": state["run_id"]},
236	        "recursion_limit": GRAPH_RECURSION_LIMIT,
237	    }
238	    with run_trace_context(state["run_id"]):
239	        result = graph.invoke(state, config=thread_config)
240	    return result
241

1	"""
2	P1 Agent — SCF NLP Characterization (STRM vs. Computational Similarity).
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Applies NLP similarity methods to every STRM mapping and compares computational
6	findings to STRM expert labels. The gap between the two is the primary finding.
7	
8	PROTECTED AGENT: Prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution.
9	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
10	
11	WORKFLOW POSITION:
12	  Embedding Agent (FAISS + Qdrant) → P1 → Statistical Analyst, QA Agent
13	
14	PHASE DISCIPLINE:
15	  Exploratory: train + val splits. All outputs prefixed EXP_. No p-value decisions.
16	  Confirmatory: test split only, after Gate 2. Every script has SAP header.
17	
18	NIST CLUSTER CONSTRAINT:
19	  NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 share authorship — treated as ONE evidence source.
20	  Cross-framework findings citing all three as independent validation are invalid.
21	
22	NLP METHOD TIERS (all must run in exploratory phase — no cherry-picking):
23	  1. Lexical: TF-IDF cosine, BM25
24	  2. Embedding: cosine similarity over dense embeddings (5 model candidates)
25	  3. Topic: LDA/BERTopic coherence correlation
26	  4. Entailment: NLI model on (source, target) pairs
27	  5. Hybrid: weighted ensemble of tiers 1-4
28	
29	See AGENT.md: agents/03-analysis/p1-strm-nlp/AGENT.md
30	"""
31	
32	from __future__ import annotations
33	
34	from pathlib import Path
35	from typing import Any

38	from ..base import BaseResearchAgent

43	    SCF NLP Characterization agent.
44

46	        - train_ids / val_ids / test_ids: split control ID lists
47	        - data_split_seed: for reproducibility logging
48	        - run_id: for MLflow experiment tagging and PROV-DM
49	
50	    Produces (written to analysis/):
51	        EXP_P1_similarity.parquet   — all-pairs similarity across NLP method tiers
52	        EXP_P1_classification.parquet — predicted relationship types vs. STRM labels
53	        EXP_P1_disagreements.md     — high-disagreement mappings for Gate 3 review
54	        H1.x_results.parquet       — confirmatory per-hypothesis results (post-Gate 2)
55	    """
56	
57	    PROTECTED = True
58	    AGENT_ID = "p1-strm-nlp"
59	
60	    def __init__(
61	        self,
62	        output_dir: Path | str = Path("analysis"),
63	        embedding_model_id: str | None = None,
64	        nist_cluster_ids: set[str] | None = None,
65	    ) -> None:
66	        self.output_dir = Path(output_dir)
67	        self.embedding_model_id = embedding_model_id
68	        # Controls from NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 — one authorship cluster
69	        self.nist_cluster_ids: set[str] = nist_cluster_ids or set()
70	
71	    # ── Exploratory phase ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72	
73	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
74	        """
75	        Run all 5 NLP method tiers on train + val splits.
76	
77	        MUST NOT access test_ids.
78	        All outputs written to analysis/01-exploratory/ with EXP_ prefix.
79	        No p-value decision language in any output.

81	        raise NotImplementedError(
82	            "P1 exploratory analysis not yet implemented. "
83	            "Phase 1 implementation will: (1) load STRM mappings from "
84	            "data/splits/train.parquet + val.parquet, (2) run all 5 NLP tiers, "
85	            "(3) write EXP_P1_similarity.parquet and EXP_P1_classification.parquet, "
86	            "(4) generate EXP_P1_disagreements.md for Gate 3."

112	    def _is_nist_cluster(self, framework: str) -> bool:

116	        NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 share authorship. Citing agreement

3

17	  subset (⊂) and superset (⊃): directed
18	  equivalence (=) and intersection (∩): undirected (add edges in both directions)
19	  strength_score: edge weight; imputed at median if missing (document in codebook)
20	
21	COMMUNITY DETECTION:
22	  Must run at least Leiden AND Louvain — results reported for both.
23	  Single-algorithm community structure is not reported as authoritative.

25	NIST CLUSTER CONSTRAINT:

41	class P2ControlTopologyAgent(BaseResearchAgent):
42	    """
43	    Control space topology agent.
44	
45	    Inputs (from state + filesystem):
46	        - STRM mappings: data/splits/train.parquet + val.parquet (exploratory)
47	        - Control metadata: ara/artifacts/ (node attributes: domain, framework, maturity)
48

50	        EXP_P2_graph.graphml         — directed + undirected graph per edge type
51	        EXP_P2_topology.parquet      — degree, betweenness centrality, clustering coeff, PageRank
52	        EXP_P2_communities.parquet   — community ID, size, intra/inter-community density
53	        EXP_P2_hubs.md               — top 50 most central controls with annotations
54	        H2.x_results.parquet         — confirmatory results (post-Gate 2)
55	    """

57	    PROTECTED = True

83	            "(2) construct directed/undirected graph per edge type, "
84	            "(3) compute centrality metrics (batch if needed for scale), "
85	            "(4) run Leiden + Louvain community detection, "

115	        self,

120	        Impute missing edge weight at median. Returns (value, was_imputed).

125	        if strength_score is None:
126	            return median_strength, True
127	        return strength_score, False
128

1	"""
2	P3 Agent — Regulatory Convergence Atlas.
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Measures pairwise cross-framework semantic and structural convergence across
6	all 33 SCF domains. Distinguishes GENUINE convergence (semantic alignment)
7	from TERMINOLOGICAL convergence (similar words, different meaning). Produces
8	the Convergence Atlas — a quantified map of where frameworks actually agree.
9	
10	PROTECTED AGENT: Prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution.
11	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
12	
13	WORKFLOW POSITION:
14	  P2 (community assignments) → P3 → P4 (convergence gaps inform blindspot detection),
15	                                     Statistical Analyst, Report Agent
16	
17	CONVERGENCE TYPES (must classify every finding):
18	  - GENUINE:          semantic alignment — embeddings agree, STRM labels confirm
19	  - TERMINOLOGICAL:   similar words, different semantic content — must never be
20	                      reported as genuine convergence
21	  - STRUCTURAL:       same position in graph topology without semantic agreement
22	
23	NIST CLUSTER CONSTRAINT (MANDATORY):
24	  NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 are ONE authorship cluster.
25	  Convergence between them is NIST_CLUSTER_INTERNAL — excluded from cross-framework
26	  convergence claims. This constraint applies to every framework pair computation.
27	
28	CLASSIFIER CONFIDENCE THRESHOLD:
29	  Convergence type classification (LLM via Instructor/Pydantic) requires
30	  confidence ≥ 0.70. Below threshold → human review flag; do not auto-classify.
31	
32	See AGENT.md: agents/03-analysis/p3-regulatory-convergence/AGENT.md
33	"""
34	
35	from __future__ import annotations
36	
37	from pathlib import Path
38	from typing import Any, Literal
39	
40	from ...state import HCGRCState
41	from ..base import BaseResearchAgent
42	
43	# Convergence classification types
44	ConvergenceType = Literal["genuine", "terminological", "structural", "nist_cluster_internal"]
45	
46	
47	class P3RegulatoryConvergenceAgent(BaseResearchAgent):
48	    """
49	    Regulatory convergence atlas agent.
50	
51	    Inputs (from state + filesystem):
52	        - P2 community assignments: analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_communities.parquet
53	        - Qdrant: framework-tagged control embeddings
54	        - STRM equivalence mappings: data/splits/ (relationship_type == "equal")
55	
56	    Produces:
57	        EXP_P3_convergence.parquet  — framework × framework × domain convergence scores
58	        EXP_P3_atlas.json           — structured atlas: genuine, terminological, divergence
59	        EXP_P3_heatmap.parquet      — input to Visualization Agent
60	        H3.x_results.parquet        — confirmatory results (post-Gate 2)
61	    """
62	
63	    PROTECTED = True
64	    AGENT_ID = "p3-regulatory-convergence"
65	
66	    # Classifier confidence threshold — below this, flag for human review
67	    CLASSIFIER_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD = 0.70
68	
69	    # False convergence prevalence threshold — above this, Gate 3 discussion item
70	    FALSE_CONVERGENCE_GATE3_THRESHOLD = 0.05  # 5%
71	
72	    def __init__(
73	        self,
74	        output_dir: Path | str = Path("analysis"),
75	        classifier_model: str | None = None,
76	    ) -> None:
77	        self.output_dir = Path(output_dir)
78	        # LLM used for convergence type classification (Instructor/Pydantic)
79	        # Must be local — no SaaS inference
80	        self.classifier_model = classifier_model
81	
82	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
83	        """
84	        Compute pairwise framework convergence across all 33 domains.
85	
86	        Must NOT access test_ids.
87	        All outputs EXP_-prefixed.
88	        NIST cluster constraint applied to every framework pair.
89	        Terminological convergence classified and never reported as genuine.
90	        """
91	        raise NotImplementedError(
92	            "P3 exploratory convergence analysis not yet implemented. "
93	            "Phase 1 implementation will: (1) load P2 community assignments, "
94	            "(2) compute pairwise cosine similarity between framework-specific "
95	            "control embeddings per domain, (3) classify convergence type via "
96	            "Instructor+Pydantic (confidence >= 0.70), (4) apply NIST cluster "
97	            "constraint to all framework pairs, (5) write convergence.parquet, "
98	            "atlas.json, heatmap.parquet."
99	        )
100	
101	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
102	        """
103	        Execute one pre-registered confirmatory convergence test on test split.
104	
105	        Gate 2 approval verified by BaseResearchAgent.__call__.
106	        Requires: state["current_hypothesis_id"] == "H3.[n]"
107	        """
108	        raise NotImplementedError(
109	            "P3 confirmatory analysis not yet implemented. "
110	            "Requires: Gate 2 approved, hypothesis_id in state."
111	        )
112	
113	    def _is_nist_cluster_pair(self, fw_a: str, fw_b: str) -> bool:
114	        """
115	        Return True if both frameworks are in the NIST authorship cluster.
116	
117	        Pairs that return True must be tagged NIST_CLUSTER_INTERNAL and
118	        excluded from cross-framework convergence claims.
119	        """
120	        nist = {"NIST 800-53", "NIST CSF", "NIST 800-171"}
121	        return fw_a.upper() in nist and fw_b.upper() in nist
122	
123	    def _classify_convergence(
124	        self,
125	        similarity_score: float,
126	        strm_relationship: str,
127	        linguistic_overlap: float,
128	    ) -> tuple[ConvergenceType, float]:
129	        """
130	        Classify convergence type and return (type, confidence).
131	
132	        In Phase 1 implementation: calls local LLM via Instructor with a
133	        typed Pydantic ConvergenceClassification output schema.
134	
135	        If confidence < CLASSIFIER_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD, returns
136	        ("terminological", confidence) and flags for human review —
137	        do NOT auto-classify uncertain cases.
138	        """
139	        raise NotImplementedError(
140	            "Convergence classification requires Instructor + local LLM. "
141	            "Not implemented until Phase 1 LLM infrastructure is available."
142	        )
143

1	"""
2	P4 Agent — Risk Blindspot Engine.
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Maps the 39 SCF risk categories against actual control coverage across all
6	framework combinations. Quantifies which risk categories are over-controlled,
7	under-controlled, or absent for a given framework set. Compliance ≠ security —
8	this agent measures the gap.
9	
10	PROTECTED AGENT: Prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution.
11	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
12	
13	WORKFLOW POSITION:
14	  P2 (topology, isolated domains) + P3 (convergence atlas, false coverage flags)
15	  → P4 → Statistical Analyst, Report Agent
16	
17	FALSE COVERAGE HANDLING:
18	  P3 flags controls that appear to cover a risk category but are classified as
19	  TERMINOLOGICAL convergence. P4 must exclude these from coverage density counts —
20	  including them would overstate coverage and understate blindspot severity.
21	
22	RISK CONSTITUTION:
23	  The 39 SCF risk categories are defined in docs/charter/RISK_CONSTITUTION.md.
24	  P4 does not define or modify risk categories — it only measures coverage
25	  against the pre-defined 39.
26	
27	FRAMEWORK COMBINATIONS:
28	  Pre-specified in configs/analysis.yaml (framework_combinations section).
29	  P4 does not select which combinations to analyze — this is pre-registered.
30	
31	See AGENT.md: agents/03-analysis/p4-risk-blindspot/AGENT.md
32	"""
33	
34	from __future__ import annotations
35	
36	from pathlib import Path
37	from typing import Any
38	
39	from ...state import HCGRCState
40	from ..base import BaseResearchAgent
41	
42	# Coverage density threshold below which a risk category is a "blindspot"
43	# Pre-registered value — do not change after Gate 2
44	BLINDSPOT_COVERAGE_THRESHOLD = 0.30  # 30% of applicable controls cover the risk
45	
46	
47	class P4RiskBlindspotAgent(BaseResearchAgent):
48	    """
49	    Risk blindspot engine agent.
50	
51	    Inputs (from state + filesystem):
52	        - Risk categories: docs/charter/RISK_CONSTITUTION.md (39 categories)
53	        - Control-to-risk mappings: ara/artifacts/ (SCF native risk assignments)
54	        - P2 topology: EXP_P2_topology.parquet (isolated domain flags)
55	        - P3 convergence: EXP_P3_atlas.json (false convergence flags)
56	        - Framework combinations: configs/analysis.yaml
57	
58	    Produces:
59	        EXP_P4_coverage.parquet      — risk category × framework combo: coverage density
60	        EXP_P4_blindspots.json       — risk categories below threshold, by framework combo
61	        EXP_P4_false_coverage.parquet — controls appearing to cover a risk but flagged by P3
62	        H4.x_results.parquet          — confirmatory results (post-Gate 2)
63	    """
64	
65	    PROTECTED = True
66	    AGENT_ID = "p4-risk-blindspot"
67	
68	    N_RISK_CATEGORIES = 39  # SCF RISK_CONSTITUTION.md — fixed, do not modify
69	
70	    def __init__(
71	        self,
72	        output_dir: Path | str = Path("analysis"),
73	        blindspot_threshold: float = BLINDSPOT_COVERAGE_THRESHOLD,
74	    ) -> None:
75	        self.output_dir = Path(output_dir)
76	        self.blindspot_threshold = blindspot_threshold
77	
78	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
79	        """
80	        Compute risk coverage matrix across all framework combinations.
81	
82	        Must NOT access test_ids.
83	        All outputs EXP_-prefixed.
84	        False coverage flags from P3 must be loaded and applied before
85	        computing coverage density — do not count terminological matches.
86	        """
87	        raise NotImplementedError(
88	            "P4 exploratory risk blindspot analysis not yet implemented. "
89	            "Phase 1 implementation will: (1) load 39 risk categories from "
90	            "RISK_CONSTITUTION.md, (2) load P3 false coverage flags, "
91	            "(3) compute coverage density per risk category × framework combination "
92	            "(excluding false coverage), (4) identify blindspots below threshold, "
93	            "(5) write coverage.parquet, blindspots.json, false_coverage.parquet."
94	        )
95	
96	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
97	        """
98	        Execute one pre-registered confirmatory blindspot test on test split.
99	
100	        Gate 2 approval verified by BaseResearchAgent.__call__.
101	        Requires: state["current_hypothesis_id"] == "H4.[n]"
102	        """
103	        raise NotImplementedError(
104	            "P4 confirmatory analysis not yet implemented. "
105	            "Requires: Gate 2 approved, hypothesis_id in state."
106	        )
107	
108	    def _exclude_false_coverage(
109	        self,
110	        control_ids: list[str],
111	        false_coverage_control_ids: set[str],
112	    ) -> list[str]:
113	        """
114	        Remove controls flagged by P3 as terminological convergence from coverage count.
115	
116	        Including these would overstate how well a framework covers a risk category.
117	        The exclusion must be logged in the codebook entry for each affected risk category.
118	        """
119	        return [cid for cid in control_ids if cid not in false_coverage_control_ids]
120	
121	    def _compute_coverage_density(
122	        self,
123	        applicable_controls: list[str],
124	        covering_controls: list[str],
125	    ) -> float:
126	        """
127	        Coverage density = covering_controls / applicable_controls.
128	
129	        Returns 0.0 if applicable_controls is empty (underpopulated risk category —
130	        log as coverage gap, not as a blindspot finding).
131	        """
132	        if not applicable_controls:
133	            return 0.0
134	        return len(set(covering_controls) & set(applicable_controls)) / len(applicable_controls)
135

1	"""
2	P5 Agent — AI Governance Cluster Analysis.
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Applies HDBSCAN+UMAP clustering to the 1,400 SCF control embeddings to discover
6	whether the official 33-domain taxonomy is empirically grounded. Does the SCF
7	domain structure reflect how controls actually cluster by semantic content and
8	co-occurrence? This is asked per-domain (33 × HDBSCAN) and cross-domain.
9	
10	PROTECTED AGENT: Prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution.
11	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
12	
13	WORKFLOW POSITION:
14	  Embedding Agent → P5 (largely independent of P1-P4 in exploratory phase)
15	  P1-P4 outputs → P5 cross-domain synthesis (confirmatory phase)
16	  → Statistical Analyst, Report Agent
17	
18	UMAP PARAMETERS:
19	  n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.1, metric=cosine (defaults in configs/platform.yaml)
20	  Parameters are tuned during exploratory phase — final values logged to MLflow
21	  before any confirmatory clustering runs. Parameter decisions are logged to
22	  the Preregistration Ledger at Gate 2 (part of the model/parameter lock).
23	
24	HDBSCAN PARAMETERS:
25	  min_cluster_size=10, min_samples=5, metric=euclidean (on UMAP-reduced embeddings)
26	  Noise points (label = -1) are reported as unclustered, not assigned to clusters.
27	
28	TAXONOMY COMPARISON:
29	  Empirical cluster assignments are compared to SCF's 33 official domains using
30	  Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) and Normalized Mutual Information (NMI). These are
31	  the primary H5.x test statistics.
32	
33	See AGENT.md: agents/03-analysis/p5-ai-governance/AGENT.md
34	"""
35	
36	from __future__ import annotations
37	
38	from pathlib import Path
39	from typing import Any
40	
41	from ...state import HCGRCState
42	from ..base import BaseResearchAgent
43	
44	N_SCF_DOMAINS = 33  # Official SCF taxonomy — fixed
45	N_SCF_CONTROLS = 1400  # Expected SCF control count per SAP §7
46	
47	
48	class P5AiGovernanceAgent(BaseResearchAgent):
49	    """
50	    AI governance cluster analysis agent.
51	
52	    Inputs (from state + filesystem):
53	        - Control embeddings: Qdrant collection hcgrc_controls (all 1,400 controls)
54	        - Control metadata: ara/artifacts/ (domain, framework, maturity, MCR/DSR)
55	        - STRM co-occurrence patterns: data/splits/ (for cross-domain synthesis)
56	        - P1-P4 outputs: used in cross-domain synthesis only (confirmatory)
57	        - SCF domain definitions: docs/charter/SCF_CONSTITUTION.md
58	
59	    Produces:
60	        EXP_P5_domain_clusters.parquet  — 33 files × (cluster_id, control_id, label)
61	        EXP_P5_cross_domain.parquet     — latent governance groups spanning domains
62	        EXP_P5_umap.parquet             — 2D + 3D UMAP projections
63	        EXP_P5_taxonomy_comparison.md   — ARI/NMI: empirical clusters vs. SCF domains
64	        H5.x_results.parquet            — confirmatory results (post-Gate 2)
65	    """
66	
67	    PROTECTED = True
68	    AGENT_ID = "p5-ai-governance"
69	
70	    def __init__(
71	        self,
72	        output_dir: Path | str = Path("analysis"),
73	        umap_n_neighbors: int = 15,
74	        umap_min_dist: float = 0.1,
75	        umap_metric: str = "cosine",
76	        hdbscan_min_cluster_size: int = 10,
77	        hdbscan_min_samples: int = 5,
78	    ) -> None:
79	        self.output_dir = Path(output_dir)
80	        # UMAP parameters — from configs/platform.yaml defaults
81	        self.umap_n_neighbors = umap_n_neighbors
82	        self.umap_min_dist = umap_min_dist
83	        self.umap_metric = umap_metric
84	        # HDBSCAN parameters — applied on UMAP-reduced embeddings
85	        self.hdbscan_min_cluster_size = hdbscan_min_cluster_size
86	        self.hdbscan_min_samples = hdbscan_min_samples
87	
88	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
89	        """
90	        Run per-domain clustering (33 × HDBSCAN) and cross-domain synthesis.
91	
92	        Must NOT access test_ids.
93	        All outputs EXP_-prefixed.
94	        UMAP parameters tuned here — final values logged to MLflow before Gate 2.
95	        Noise points (HDBSCAN label = -1) reported as unclustered, never force-assigned.
96	        """
97	        raise NotImplementedError(
98	            "P5 exploratory cluster analysis not yet implemented. "
99	            "Phase 1 implementation will: (1) load all 1,400 control embeddings "
100	            "from Qdrant, (2) run UMAP dimensionality reduction, (3) run HDBSCAN "
101	            "per domain (33 runs) + cross-domain, (4) compute ARI/NMI against "
102	            "SCF 33-domain taxonomy, (5) write domain_clusters.parquet, "
103	            "cross_domain.parquet, umap.parquet, taxonomy_comparison.md."
104	        )
105	
106	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
107	        """
108	        Execute one pre-registered confirmatory clustering test on test split embeddings.
109	
110	        Gate 2 approval verified by BaseResearchAgent.__call__.
111	        Requires: state["current_hypothesis_id"] == "H5.[n]"
112	        UMAP + HDBSCAN parameters locked at Gate 2 — must not re-tune on test split.
113	        """
114	        raise NotImplementedError(
115	            "P5 confirmatory analysis not yet implemented. "
116	            "Requires: Gate 2 approved, parameters locked in state, hypothesis_id set."
117	        )
118	
119	    def _report_noise_points(
120	        self,
121	        cluster_labels: list[int],
122	        control_ids: list[str],
123	    ) -> tuple[list[str], float]:
124	        """
125	        Return (noise_control_ids, noise_fraction).
126	
127	        HDBSCAN label = -1 indicates noise (no cluster assignment).
128	        Noise points are reported separately — do not assign to nearest cluster.
129	        High noise fraction (> 0.20) is a finding, not a failure.
130	        """
131	        noise_ids = [cid for cid, label in zip(control_ids, cluster_labels) if label == -1]
132	        noise_fraction = len(noise_ids) / len(control_ids) if control_ids else 0.0
133	        return noise_ids, noise_fraction

1	"""
2	reasoning_client — the ADR-0016 LLM execution-routing seam.
3	
4	A single abstraction that isolates LLM auth/transport from agent logic. Agents
5	declare *what* to reason about and at *what stakes tier*; this seam decides
6	*where* the call runs, how it authenticates, how it retries, times out, and how
7	it is traced. No agent imports an LLM client directly — that coupling lives here
8	and only here (ADR-0016 §"Implementation seam").
9	
10	Tiers (routing is by stakes, per ADR-0016 / agents/MODEL_TIER_ASSIGNMENTS.md):
11	
12	    Tier.T1  — Deterministic ML. No LLM. complete() on T1 is a routing bug and
13	               raises: T1 work is computation, never inference.
14	    Tier.T2  — Local LLM on the Mac mini, 24/7 (Ollama). Bulk / low-stakes
15	               reasoning: per-pair classification, triage, drafting, review.
16	    Tier.T3  — Frontier Claude Max 20x via claude-agent-sdk. The `claude` CLI's
17	               subscription OAuth (CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN) is the ONLY auth path
18	               — NO Anthropic API key. ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is scrubbed from the SDK
19	               subprocess env so a stray key can never spill to the metered API
20	               (ADR-0016). Reserved for high-stakes judgments. Bursty / queued.
21	
22	Reliability (hardening pass 1):
23	  - Every call has a per-tier timeout (#145, #158) so a hung backend can never
24	    block a synchronous LangGraph node forever.
25	  - Transient failures are retried with bounded exponential backoff (#157).
26	  - Every call is wrapped in an OpenTelemetry span carrying run_id/agent_id/tier
27	    (OpenInference LLM conventions) so BOTH tiers are visible in Phoenix — T3 is
28	    not LangChain-instrumented, so without this it would be invisible (#150, #168).
29	    Spans are cheap no-ops when no tracer provider is registered (offline/tests).
30	  - complete_json uses Ollama's native JSON mode on T2 and a balanced-brace
31	    extractor, instead of a greedy regex that mangled multi-value replies (#159).
32	
33	Data sovereignty (ADR-0002 as amended by ADR-0016): persistent artifacts never
34	leave the machine; T3 permits transient, non-retained inference over the specific
35	SCF-derived text needed for a single call.
36	
37	Public surface:
38	    complete(tier, prompt, system=..., run_id=..., agent_id=...) -> str
39	    complete_json(tier, prompt, ...) -> dict | list
40	    is_available(tier) -> bool
41	"""
42	
43	from __future__ import annotations
44	
45	import asyncio
46	import json
47	import os
48	import threading
49	import time
50	from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
51	from concurrent.futures import TimeoutError as FuturesTimeout
52	from enum import Enum
53	from functools import lru_cache
54	from typing import Any
55	
56	from opentelemetry import trace
57	
58	# ── Tiers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
59	
60	
61	class Tier(str, Enum):
62	    """Execution tier, by stakes. See agents/MODEL_TIER_ASSIGNMENTS.md."""
63	
64	    T1 = "t1"  # deterministic ML — no LLM
65	    T2 = "t2"  # local Ollama
66	    T3 = "t3"  # frontier Claude Max via Agent SDK
67	
68	
69	class ReasoningError(RuntimeError):
70	    """Raised when a reasoning call cannot be completed (backend down, timeout, T1 misuse)."""
71	
72	
73	class PermanentReasoningError(ReasoningError):
74	    """A non-transient failure (model missing, auth, CLI absent). Never retried."""
75	
76	
77	class RateLimitError(ReasoningError):
78	    """A Tier-3 subscription rate window. Retried with long backpressure, never
79	    spilled to a metered API (ADR-0016)."""
80	
81	
82	# ── Configuration (env-overridable; local-first defaults) ─────────────────────
83	
84	
85	def _env(name: str, default: str) -> str:
86	    val = os.environ.get(name, "").strip()
87	    return val or default
88	
89	
90	T2_MODEL = _env("HCGRC_T2_MODEL", "llama3.1:8b")
91	T2_BASE_URL = _env("HCGRC_OLLAMA_BASE_URL", "http://localhost:11434")
92	T3_MODEL = os.environ.get("HCGRC_T3_MODEL", "").strip() or None
93	
94	# Per-tier hard timeouts (seconds). T3 is bursty/queued so it gets more headroom.
95	# Clamped to a positive floor: a zero/negative override would raise ValueError
96	# ("Timeout value out of range") at future.result()/asyncio.wait_for() (pass-11),
97	# mirroring the backoff/retry clamps above.
98	T2_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("HCGRC_T2_TIMEOUT", "120")))
99	T3_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("HCGRC_T3_TIMEOUT", "300")))
100

1	# ADR-0012: Autonomous Iterative Research Architecture
2	
3	**Status:** Accepted  
4	**Date:** 2026-06-10  
5	**Author:** [REDACTED]  
6	**Supersedes:** N/A  
7	**Relates to:** ADR-0010 (three-tier program), ADR-0011 (resilience), AGENT_WORKFLOW.md
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Context
12	
13	The platform was initially designed as a linear research pipeline: one pass through phases 0–5, five pre-specified analysis modules (P1–P5), terminating at a manuscript. This assumed a human researcher making deliberate analytical choices at each phase.
14	
15	The actual design intent is different. Thomas builds and governs the infrastructure. The platform conducts the research — autonomously theorizing, iterating, self-correcting, and evolving its direction. Thomas approves hard pivots; the platform handles everything else without waiting for human input.
16	
17	This ADR captures that design intent and the architecture it requires.
18	
19	---
20	
21	## Decision
22	
23	The HC-GRC platform is an **autonomous research organism**, not a research pipeline.
24	
25	Key architectural commitments:
26	
27	1. **Cyclic topology over linear pipeline** — exploratory findings feed back into hypothesis generation; new research threads spawn and execute without requiring a full restart
28	2. **Two-tier autonomy** — tangential directions proceed autonomously; substantially different directions route to human approval before execution
29	3. **Platform-determined publication cadence** — the platform researches optimal publication cadence, proposes a roadmap, and Thomas approves the proposal (not each publication decision)
30	4. **Evolving module set** — P1–P5 are initial hypotheses, not fixed scope; the platform generates, pursues, and retires modules as evidence warrants
31	
32	---
33	
34	## The Research Charter
35	
36	The platform maintains a **Research Charter** — a structured, machine-readable statement of the current approved research direction. It captures:
37	
38	```
39	{
40	  "primary_objective": "Understand the SCF data space empirically — inputs, outputs, mappings, deviations, financial risk implications",
41	  "approved_domains": [...],      // subject areas currently in scope
42	  "methodology_class": [...],     // analytical approaches currently approved
43	  "data_sources": [...],          // licensed data sources currently available
44	  "approved_tier": 1,             // current program tier
45	  "compass_version": "1.0.0"      // version-controlled; changes require Thomas approval
46	}
47	```
48	
49	The Charter is the reference point for drift detection. It is version-controlled and each approved version is timestamped. Thomas approves Charter changes, not individual analysis decisions.
50	
51	---
52	
53	## Autonomy Model — Autonomous vs. Escalation
54	
55	### Autonomous — Platform Proceeds
56	
57	The platform proceeds without human approval when a proposed new direction is **tangential** to the current Charter:
58	
59	- New sub-hypotheses that refine a question already in scope
60	- Method variations within an approved methodology class
61	- New analytical angles on existing approved data sources
62	- Exploratory threads that emerge from findings within an approved domain
63	- Failure recovery and self-repair that stays within current scope
64	
65	**Operational definition:** A direction is tangential if it does not require a Charter amendment — no new domains, no new methodology classes, no new data sources, no tier change.
66	
67	All Autonomous actions are logged with full provenance. Thomas can review the log at any time; the platform does not wait for review before proceeding.
68	
69	### Escalation — Operator Approval Required
70	
71	The platform parks the proposed direction and notifies Thomas when it would require a **Charter amendment**:
72	
73	- New subject domains not in the current Charter
74	- Substantially different methodology classes (e.g., shifting from NLP similarity to causal inference)
75	- New data sources requiring licensing or privacy review
76	- Tier escalation (Tier 1 → Tier 2, Tier 2 → Tier 3)
77	- Any action with irreversible consequences outside current scope
78	
79	**Notification format:** The platform generates a structured proposal containing: proposed Charter amendment, rationale from findings, estimated scope and compute impact, recommended approval/rejection, and the finding that triggered it. Thomas responds with approve/reject/defer.
80	
81	**Non-negotiable:** Escalation never auto-approves. The platform waits indefinitely for an Escalation decision. It continues all Autonomous work in parallel. Thomas does not slow down innovation — he gates scope expansion, not execution within approved scope.
82	
83	---
84	
85	## Feedback Loop Architecture
86	
87	The current linear topology (phases 0→5, terminate) is replaced with a cyclic structure:
88	
89	```
90	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
91	│                  RESEARCH ORGANISM                  │
92	│                                                     │
93	│   Data Ingestion → Exploratory Analysis             │
94	│         ↑                    ↓                      │
95	│   Hypothesis             Findings                   │
96	│   Generation  ←─────────────┘                      │
97	│         ↓                                           │
98	│   Drift Detection                                   │
99	│    ↙          ↘                                     │
100	│ Autonomous   Escalation                                │
101	│ (proceed)     (park + notify Thomas)                │
102	│    ↓                                                │
103	│ Confirmatory Analysis                               │
104	│         ↓                                           │
105	│   Publication Trigger Evaluation                    │
106	│    ↙          ↘                                     │
107	│ Publish       Continue                              │
108	│               (loop back to Exploratory)            │
109	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
110	```
111	
112	The graph does not terminate after one pass. Each completed analysis cycle evaluates whether publication criteria are met. If not, findings feed back into the next exploratory cycle.
113	
114	---
115	
116	## Publication Trigger
117	
118	Publication cadence is platform-determined, not pre-specified. The platform:
119	
120	1. Researches optimal publication cadence for empirical GRC/NLP work (venues, timing, preprint vs. peer review norms)
121	2. Proposes a publication roadmap as a Charter amendment (Escalation — Thomas approves)
122	3. Once approved, evaluates publication readiness after each completed confirmatory cycle
123	4. Triggers manuscript preparation when readiness criteria are met
124	
125	**Readiness criteria (platform-evaluated):**
126	- Minimum evidentiary threshold met for at least one primary finding
127	- Null results documented with equal rigor to positive findings
128	- Reproducibility package complete
129	- Finding is novel relative to published literature (platform monitors continuously)
130	
131	---
132	
133	## Implications for Pre-Registration
134	
135	The traditional pre-registration model (register hypotheses before data is seen, never deviate) does not fit a continuously iterating autonomous system. This does not mean abandoning epistemic rigor — it means applying a different governance model appropriate to the execution model.
136	
137	Adopted approach: **Registered Reports with continuous amendment logging**
138	
139	- Each hypothesis the platform pursues is logged to the Preregistration Ledger with a timestamp before confirmatory testing begins — regardless of whether it was pre-specified or platform-generated
140	- The audit trail in W3C PROV-DM provides complete lineage from observation → hypothesis → test → result
141	- The platform cannot retroactively alter a logged hypothesis or its test result
142	- Exploratory findings are always distinguished from confirmatory findings in all outputs
143	
144	This provides stronger transparency guarantees than traditional pre-registration because every step is logged in real time, not just the initial hypothesis set.
145	
146	---
147	
148	## Consequences
149	
150	**What this enables:**

1	# ADR-0015: Architecture Integrity Decisions
2	
3	**Status:** Accepted  
4	**Date:** 2026-06-10  
5	**Author:** [REDACTED]  
6	**Supersedes:** N/A  
7	**Relates to:** ADR-0011 (resilience), ADR-0012 (autonomous architecture), ADR-0014 (infrastructure)
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Context
12	
13	Adversarial review surfaced ten architecture integrity issues (#71–#80) spanning: structural firewall implementation, orchestrator failure model, state schema safety, data split integrity, checkpoint scale, agent evolution scope, inter-agent trust, observability correlation, and gate delivery mechanism. This ADR captures the resolution of all ten.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## Decisions
18	
19	### 1. Structural Firewall Claims Are Conditional on Phase 0 (#71)
20	
21	All claims that "the firewall between phases is structural, not procedural" — in ADR-0004, ADR-0007, README, and the Charter — are conditional on Phase 0 implementation completing before any data acquisition.
22	
23	**Phase 0 success criteria (hard prerequisites, added to GATES.md):**
24	- LangGraph skeleton running with all gate nodes present
25	- Gate 1 firing with synthetic payload confirmed
26	- PostgresSaver checkpointing confirmed on target hardware
27	- Gate 2 data split logic implemented and idempotency test passing
28	- No SCF data acquired until all criteria are met
29	
30	The structural firewall claim becomes true when Phase 0 completes. Not before.
31	
32	---
33	
34	### 2. Orchestrator Failure Model — Option B + Evolve Layer (#72)
35	
36	**Option B — Defensive gate nodes:** Each gate validates its own preconditions and rejects invalid state regardless of how T-00 routed. Gate nodes are the enforcement surface.
37	
38	**Evolve layer:** Gate rejection events are logged with full context (proposed action, rejection reason, state snapshot, run_id, timestamp) to a `failure_events` store. Agent Evolution monitors `failure_events` for patterns:
39	
40	- **Routing errors** (T-00 operational): Agent Evolution applies DSPy re-optimization to T-00's routing prompt autonomously. Logged to PROV-DM with before/after versions.
41	- **Architectural failures** (gate topology wrong, systematic design flaw): Escalation filed to operator.
42	
43	This makes the platform self-correcting for operational routing failures without requiring a permanent watchdog node. Option A (real-time watchdog) becomes redundant once T-00's routing stabilizes through the evolve loop.
44	
45	**Protected agent set:** Agent Evolution may not autonomously modify P1–P5, statistical-analyst, or hypothesis-formalizer prompts. See Decision 6.
46	
47	---
48	
49	### 3. Provenance Entry Ordering (#73)
50	
51	Every entry appended to `prov_trace`, `errors`, and `null_results` carries:
52	- `timestamp`: ISO 8601 UTC, generated at write time by the writing agent
53	- `source_id`: agent name + run_id + superstep number
54	
55	Downstream consumers sort by `timestamp` before processing. List position is never used as a proxy for execution order. Enforced by ARA Rigor Reviewer at validation time.
56	
57	---
58	
59	### 4. Gate Status Write Protection (#74)
60	
61	All `gate_status` writes go through `gate_coordinator_node`. No other node writes this key directly.
62	
63	**Phase 0 (linear topology) — implemented via merge reducer:**
64	`gate_status` is typed `Annotated[dict[GateID, GateStatus], lambda a, b: {**a, **b}]`. Each `gate_coordinator_node` call returns only the new `{gate_id: record}` entry; LangGraph merges it into the existing dict. This is safe under concurrent writes because dict-merge is commutative for non-overlapping keys (each gate writes a unique `gate_id`).
65	
66	`gate_coordinator_node` is a helper function called from within gate nodes — it is **not** a standalone LangGraph node in Phase 0. The ADR was written before implementation; the merge reducer approach was selected during adversarial review (F1/F4, 2026-06-11) as the correct Phase 0 mechanism.
67	
68	**Phase 1 (parallel topology) — to be implemented:**
69	When Phase 1 introduces parallel agent execution, the merge reducer alone cannot prevent two agents writing the same `gate_id` key simultaneously. Phase 1 will promote `gate_coordinator_node` to a true serialized LangGraph node with explicit edges from all gate-writing agents. Tracked in GitHub issue #103.
70	
71	**Topology enforcement (Decision 7 / #78) is a Phase 1 goal, not a Phase 0 guarantee.**
72	
73	---
74	
75	### 5. Data Split Idempotency — Deterministic Seed (#75)
76	
77	`compute_data_split()` derives its random seed exclusively from SHA-256 of the data manifest hash. No system entropy is used (no time-based seed, `os.urandom()`, or unseeded numpy random).
78	
79	**Required test (Phase 0 success criterion):** Run `compute_data_split()` twice with identical state. Assert bit-identical output. This test must pass before Gate 2 is considered operational.
80	
81	---
82	
83	### 6. Checkpoint Scale — Phase 0 Benchmark (#76)
84	
85	Hardware benchmarking is a Phase 0 deliverable, not a Phase 3 decision. Before Phase 3 implementation begins:
86	
87	1. Estimate checkpoint size per `HCGRCState` at analysis time
88	2. Benchmark PostgresSaver write throughput on Mac Mini compute node
89	3. Derive maximum feasible parallel branch count
90	4. Select `batch_size` accordingly — lock to SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md
91	
92	`batch_size=50` is a placeholder until the benchmark runs. The result may require architectural adjustment.
93	
94	---
95	
96	### 7. Agent Evolution Scope — Protected Agent Set (#77)
97	
98	**Protected agents** (prompt modifications require Escalation approval before taking effect):
99	
100	| Agent | Reason |
101	|-------|--------|
102	| p1-strm-nlp | Defines NLP similarity measurement approach |
103	| p2-domain-clustering | Defines clustering methodology |
104	| p3-coverage-diffusion | Defines semantic coverage measurement |
105	| p4-gap-analysis | Defines gap analysis methodology |
106	| p5-risk-quantification | Defines risk quantification approach |
107	| statistical-analyst | Defines how evidence is weighted and tested |
108	| hypothesis-formalizer | Defines how hypotheses are structured for testing |
109	
110	Modifying these agents' prompts mid-tier against SCF corpus data is analytically equivalent to changing the research design post-hoc — a form of data snooping that bypasses Gate 2.
111	
112	**Legitimate evolution paths for protected agents:**
113	
114	1. **Before Gate 1:** DSPy optimization against synthetic data or public benchmarks only. No SCF data in system yet.
115	2. **Mid-tier via Escalation:** Platform files structured proposal with specific change and rationale. Operator reviews via Claude. Decision logged before change takes effect.
116	3. **Between tiers:** Post-tier retrospective informs deliberate redesign. Changes committed and version-bumped before next tier starts. This is science working correctly — Tier 1 findings inform Tier 2 instrument design.
117	
118	**Agent Evolution scope:** Fully autonomous for all non-protected agents (T-00 routing, ingestion, reporting, QA, infrastructure).
119	
120	---
121	
122	### 8. Inter-Agent Trust — Topology Enforcement (#78)
123	
124	**Phase 1 — Option A (structural):** LangGraph edge definitions control which agents connect to which state keys. An agent not wired to write a particular key cannot reach it — enforced at graph compile time, zero runtime overhead.
125	
126	**Phase 2 — Option B (runtime assertions):** Deferred. Tracked in sub-issue #96. Activate when: a state corruption event occurs that topology enforcement did not catch, or Tier 2 scale requires finer-grained write control.
127	
128	---
129	
130	### 9. Observability Correlation — Common run_id (#79)
131	
132	A single `run_id` is generated at the start of each research run and propagated to all four observability stores:
133	
134	| Store | Integration |
135	|-------|------------|
136	| MLflow | `run_id` as experiment tag |
137	| Phoenix | `run_id` as trace attribute |
138	| W3C PROV-DM | `run_id` as activity identifier |
139	| PostgresSaver | `run_id` as thread_id / checkpoint key |
140	
141	Cross-store correlation is a single-key lookup. A `RUNBOOK.md` documents the explicit cross-store query sequence for common diagnostic scenarios. No custom query infrastructure built.
142	
143	---
144	
145	### 10. Gate 2 Delivery — Escalation Loop (#80)
146	
147	Gate 2 interrupt uses the Escalation loop established in ADR-0014. No separate UI required.
148	
149	The Gate 2 payload (split parameters, summary statistics) travels via GitHub issue — not raw SCF data. Data stays on the compute node. The operator reviews summary artifacts, not the dataset. Local-first constraint (ADR-0002) maintained.
150

1	# ADR-0016: LLM Execution Routing — Local-First Compute, Subscription Frontier Reasoning
2	
3	**Status:** Accepted (owner sign-off 2026-06-12)
4	**Date:** 2026-06-12
5	**Author:** [REDACTED]
6	**Supersedes:** N/A
7	**Amends:** ADR-0002 (local-first, data-sovereign architecture)
8	**Relates to:** ADR-0010 (three-tier program), ADR-0012 (autonomous architecture), ADR-0015 (#80 gate delivery)
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Context
13	
14	The platform's agents require LLM inference of two very different characters: (1) high-volume, latency-tolerant parsing and classification across 1,400+ controls and ~280,000 STRM mappings, and (2) a small number of high-stakes reasoning judgments where a wrong answer corrupts the science or a public output (hypothesis formalization, genuine-vs-terminological convergence, adversarial QA, statistical interpretation, manuscript reasoning).
15	
16	A single backend cannot serve both well. Routing all work to a frontier model is unaffordable and unnecessary; routing all work to a local model under-serves the judgments that carry research-integrity risk.
17	
18	Two hard constraints frame the decision:
19	
20	1. **No metered API spend.** Execution runs entirely within a fixed **Claude Max 20x subscription ($200/month)**. The pay-per-token Anthropic API is out of scope by owner decision. This is an ordinary, fixed budget constraint — the kind every program operates under — and the orchestration framework (ADR-0001, LangGraph) is expected to plan within it.
21	2. **Dedicated headless compute.** A dedicated Mac mini runs 24/7. Latency is acceptable; bursty, queued execution against subscription rate windows is acceptable.
22	
23	ADR-0002 established local-first, data-sovereign infrastructure, motivated by the SCF's CC BY-ND license and reproducibility integrity. That ADR addressed **storage** of derived artifacts in SaaS. It did not contemplate **transient inference** over SCF-derived text by a hosted frontier model. This ADR resolves that gap explicitly.
24	
25	## Decision
26	
27	LLM work is routed across three tiers **by stakes**, not by whether a task is "reasoning."
28	
29	### Tier 1 — Deterministic ML (Mac mini, 24/7, no LLM)
30	Embeddings and the non-generative analytical modules: P2 control topology, P4 risk-coverage density, P5 clustering (HDBSCAN/UMAP). These are computation, not inference.
31	
32	### Tier 2 — Local LLM (Mac mini, 24/7)
33	Low-impact, high-volume reasoning served by a locally hosted open model (Ollama / llama.cpp): P1 per-pair STRM classification at scale, literature first-pass triage, ideation, routine documentation generation. Fully on-device; no ADR-0002 tension.
34	
35	### Tier 3 — Frontier reasoning (Claude Max 20x via the Claude Agent SDK)
36	Reserved for high-risk, high-complexity judgments only:
37	
38	| Agent | Why frontier |
39	|-------|--------------|
40	| hypothesis-formalizer | Defines the pre-registered tests; errors propagate into the entire confirmatory phase |
41	| p3-regulatory-convergence | Genuine-vs-terminological distinction is a nuanced semantic judgment |
42	| qa-agent | Adversarial critique that catches hallucinated or unsupported findings |
43	| statistical-analyst (interpretation) | Translating results into claims under SAP constraints |
44	| ml-paper-agent / whitepaper-agent (final reasoning) | Public-facing scientific claims |
45	
46	Authentication is via `claude setup-token` (a long-lived token in `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`) on the headless node, drawing the subscription's included usage. **No Anthropic API key is provisioned.**
47	
48	### Budget as a design constraint
49	The $200/month subscription envelope and its rate windows are treated as a fixed input the system designs against — not as a risk to be re-surfaced. Frontier volume is kept small by routing all low-stakes work to Tiers 1–2. When a subscription rate window is reached, frontier work **queues and resumes**; it never spills to a metered API. This backpressure is ordinary engineering within the LangGraph orchestration layer, consistent with the resilience model in ADR-0011.
50	
51	### Implementation seam
52	Tier-3 nodes call frontier inference through a thin `reasoning_client` abstraction (Agent SDK implementation). The abstraction isolates the auth/transport choice from agent logic so the platform is not coupled to a single mechanism.
53	
54	## Sovereignty position (amendment to ADR-0002)
55	
56	ADR-0002 disqualified SaaS for **storing** derived artifacts (traces, embeddings, datasets). This ADR permits **transient, non-retained inference** over SCF-derived text by the frontier model, under these conditions:
57	
58	- No derived **dataset** is transmitted or stored off-device in bulk; only the specific control/mapping text needed for a single reasoning call is sent, transiently, per call.
59	- All persistent artifacts (embeddings, traces, experiment logs, results) remain on-device per ADR-0002, unchanged.
60	- SCF attribution obligations (CC BY-ND) are preserved; no derived dataset is published.
61	- This is consistent in spirit with ADR-0015 §10, where Gate 2 payloads travel as summaries while the dataset stays on the compute node — here, the boundary is drawn at *storage*, with inference explicitly permitted.
62	
63	## Alternatives Considered
64	
65	**All-frontier routing:** Rejected — unaffordable within the fixed envelope and unnecessary for deterministic and high-volume work.
66	
67	**All-local routing:** Rejected — under-serves the high-stakes judgments (hypothesis design, convergence semantics, adversarial QA) where local model quality is a research-integrity risk.
68	
69	**Metered Anthropic API for Tier 3:** Out of scope by owner decision. The fixed-subscription model is a deliberate budget choice, not a limitation to engineer around.
70	
71	## Consequences
72	
73	**Positive:**
74	- Costs are bounded and predictable by construction (fixed subscription).
75	- The Mac mini's 24/7 capacity absorbs the heavy, deterministic majority of the workload.
76	- Frontier capability is applied precisely where correctness risk is highest.
77	- The `reasoning_client` seam prevents lock-in to any single auth/transport mechanism.
78	
79	**Negative / accepted trade-offs:**
80	- Frontier work is bursty and subject to subscription rate windows; wall-clock latency on Tier-3-heavy phases is higher. Accepted (80/20 rigor-over-speed posture).
81	- ADR-0002's "no SCF-derived content leaves the machine" stance is narrowed: transient inference is now permitted while storage remains local-only. This is the substantive change requiring owner sign-off.
82	
83	**Documents requiring update on acceptance:**
84	- ADR-0002: add cross-reference noting the inference carve-out.
85	- Agent cards: add a `model:`/tier assignment field (Tier 1/2/3) across all cards — currently unspecified.
86	- SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md: add `reasoning_client` + local serving as Phase 0/1 build items.
87	- PROJECT_CHARTER.md tech stack: record local serving (Ollama/llama.cpp) and Agent-SDK frontier path.
88	
89	## Open items (non-blocking)
90	
91	- Selection of the local open model(s) for Tier 2 — benchmarked on the mini before P1 implementation.
92	- Per-agent confirmation of Tier 2 vs Tier 3 placement as cards gain `model:` assignments.
93

      ↓                    → DataStewardAgent → EmbeddingAgent (sequential)
  exploratory_phase      ← P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P5 (sequential now;
      ↓                    TODO: parallelize via Send() when implementations live)
  hypothesis_formalize   ← HypothesisFormalizerAgent
      ↓
  gate_2                 ← (in gates.py)
      ↓
  [approved: confirmatory_entry | rejected: exploratory_phase loop]

Parallelization note:
  P1-P5 are currently called sequentially within exploratory_phase_node.
  When implementations are live, replace with Send()-based fan-out:
    def dispatch_exploratory(state) -> list[Send]:
        return [Send("p1_node", state), Send("p2_node", state), ...]
  Each worker appends to eda_artifacts and eda_agent_statuses via append reducers.
  After all workers complete, route to hypothesis_formalize.
  See LangGraph map-reduce pattern for the fan-in wiring.

NotImplementedError handling:
  Each P1-P5 call is wrapped in try/except NotImplementedError. Agents that
  have not been implemented yet record a "stub_pending" status to eda_agent_statuses.
  This allows the graph to execute in tests and governance dry-runs without

def _utc_now() -> str:
    return datetime.datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc).isoformat()


def _stub_status(agent_id: str, run_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Status record written when an agent has not been implemented yet."""
    return {
        "agent_id": agent_id,
        "status": "stub_pending",
        "note": (
            f"{agent_id} implementation pending — NotImplementedError caught. "
            "Will run when Phase 1 is live on Mac mini."
        ),
        "timestamp_utc": _utc_now(),
        "run_id": run_id,
    }


def _failed_status(agent_id: str, run_id: str, exc: BaseException) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Status record written when an agent raises a real runtime error (not a stub).

    Produces the 'failed' status the state schema documents, so one agent's bug is

    return {

1	# Methods Scaffolding
2	
3	**Lock status:** LOCKED at framework pre-registration. This document locks the methodological rules that govern the full analysis — test families, split strategy, seed management, the exploratory/confirmatory firewall, and hyperparameter handling. It does not name specific hypotheses or pre-specify effect sizes; those are deferred to the SAP. What is locked here cannot be changed after pre-registration without a documented SAP amendment.
4	
5	**Deferred:** Specific statistical tests per hypothesis, effect size thresholds, primary/secondary outcome designations. These require exploratory characterization to specify meaningfully.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. Data Split Strategy
10	
11	### Proportions
12	- **Train:** 70%
13	- **Validation:** 15%
14	- **Test (confirmatory holdout):** 15%
15	
16	Proportions are fixed. Post-registration changes to proportions constitute a SAP violation.
17	
18	### Stratification
19	Stratification variables are determined at data acquisition based on the actual distribution of the corpus. At minimum: SCF domain (33 classes) and STRM relationship type (5 classes). If domain × relationship type produces sparse cells, stratification falls back to domain only. The stratification decision is logged in the data steward's artifact before split execution.
20	
21	### Seed Management
22	The random seed for all split operations is a single value set at first data acquisition and recorded in two places: the data steward's DVC artifact metadata and the pre-registration commit. **The seed is sacrosanct after pre-registration.** Changing it after the split is executed constitutes a SAP violation equivalent to running multiple splits and selecting the favorable one.
23	
24	Seed selection method: generated from the RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit (timestamp → SHA-256 → first 8 hex digits → integer). This makes the seed auditable and eliminates any possibility of seed selection bias.
25	
26	### Test Set Access Control
27	The test split is physically inaccessible before Gate 2 (Orchestrator-enforced, LangGraph topology). The Data Steward agent writes the test split to an encrypted DVC artifact at split time. The decryption key is held by the Orchestrator and released only at Gate 2. All pre-Gate 2 evaluation runs — including model training, fine-tuning decisions, and exploratory analysis — use train + validation only.
28	
29	Any analysis that touches the test split before Gate 2 invalidates the confirmatory analysis. This is not recoverable.
30	
31	---
32	
33	## 2. Exploratory / Confirmatory Firewall
34	
35	### The Firewall Rule
36	A statistical finding is either exploratory or confirmatory. It cannot be both. An exploratory finding that is later "confirmed" by re-testing on the same data is not a confirmatory finding — it is a double-dipped exploratory finding reported with false confirmatory framing. The split architecture enforces this structurally: exploratory runs on train + validation, confirmatory runs on test.
37	
38	### What Counts as Exploratory
39	- Any analysis run before SAP lock
40	- Any analysis that was not pre-specified at SAP lock, regardless of when it runs
41	- Any analysis motivated by looking at exploratory results
42	- Hyperparameter search and calibration
43	- Model selection and architecture decisions
44	
45	### What Counts as Confirmatory
46	- Tests of pre-specified hypotheses (H[module].[n] format, locked in SAP)
47	- Run on the test split
48	- Run after Gate 2
49	- Exactly one test per hypothesis — no re-testing after seeing results
50	
51	### Reporting Rules
52	Exploratory findings are reported with descriptive statistics, visualizations, and directional language. No p-values, no significance claims, no "the data shows" framing for exploratory results. Confirmatory findings include: the pre-specified hypothesis text, the test used, the test statistic, the p-value (uncorrected and corrected), the effect size with confidence interval, and whether the pre-specified threshold was met.
53	
54	Null results in confirmatory analysis are reported with the same structure and in the same section of any output as positive results.
55	
56	---
57	
58	## 3. Test Families by Module
59	
60	These are the families of statistical methods appropriate for each module's question type. Specific tests — which variant, which correction method, what effect size threshold — are deferred to the SAP after exploratory characterization establishes the actual data structure.
61	
62	### P1 — STRM NLP Calibration
63	*Question type: classification accuracy, semantic similarity distribution*
64	
65	Applicable test families:
66	- **Classification metrics:** Precision, recall, F1 (macro and weighted), Matthews Correlation Coefficient. Primary evaluation metric chosen at SAP lock after class balance is characterized.
67	- **Distributional comparison:** Comparing semantic similarity distributions across mapping type categories. Non-parametric tests preferred (Kruskal-Wallis, Dunn post-hoc with correction) given unknown distribution shape. Parametric alternatives if normality holds.
68	- **Correlation analysis:** Rank correlation (Spearman) between NLP-derived similarity scores and expert strength scores. Pearson if both distributions are normal.
69	- **NIST cluster independence constraint:** NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 mappings are treated as a single evidence cluster. Within-cluster mappings are excluded from cross-framework analyses. This constraint is enforced at the data layer, not at the analysis layer — it is not a choice made per analysis, it is a standing rule.
70	
71	### P2 — Control Space Topology
72	*Question type: graph structure, community detection*
73	
74	Applicable test families:
75	- **Graph topology metrics:** Degree distribution, betweenness centrality, eigenvector centrality, clustering coefficient. Computed on the directed STRM graph.
76	- **Community detection:** Leiden algorithm (primary). Louvain (secondary, for comparison). Resolution parameter search is exploratory; final parameter pre-specified at SAP lock.
77	- **Community-domain comparison:** Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) between empirical community assignment and SCF domain labels. Tests whether empirical structure replicates declared domain structure.
78	- **Statistical significance of topological claims:** Permutation tests comparing observed metrics against null model (degree-preserving random graph). Specific null model variant pre-specified at SAP lock.
79	
80	### P3 — Regulatory Convergence Atlas
81	*Question type: cross-framework similarity, cluster comparison*
82	
83	Applicable test families:
84	- **Pairwise convergence quantification:** Mean semantic similarity of STRM-linked control pairs per framework dyad. Effect size: Cohen's d for comparison across dyads.
85	- **Genuine vs. terminological convergence:** Controlling for within-author-community vocabulary similarity. Specific operationalization deferred to SAP — requires observing the actual within-author vs. cross-author similarity gap in exploratory analysis.
86	- **NIST cluster independence:** As in P1 — within-NIST-cluster convergence is not treated as independent evidence of cross-framework convergence.
87	- **Multiple comparisons correction:** Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg. Method pre-specified at SAP lock after the number of framework dyads being tested is known from exploratory analysis.
88	
89	### P4 — Risk Blindspot Engine
90	*Question type: coverage distribution, gap detection*
91	
92	Applicable test families:
93	- **Coverage distribution analysis:** Distribution of control counts per risk category. Summary statistics: mean, median, Gini coefficient (coverage inequality), identified zero-coverage categories.
94	- **Gap characterization:** Risk categories below a coverage threshold (threshold pre-specified at SAP lock based on exploratory distribution). Not a null hypothesis test — gap detection is descriptive.
95	- **Domain-gap co-location:** Chi-square or Fisher's exact test for association between domain membership and below-threshold risk category coverage.
96	
97	### P5 — AI Governance Clustering
98	*Question type: clustering, structural integration*
99
